coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocurrent density of HTM-free perovskite solar cells by carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:issueName: 2nd International Workshop on Graphene and C3N4-based Photocatalysts
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Haiyuan
Guo, Daipeng
He, Bowen
Yu, Jiaguo
Fan, Ke
coredata.dc:description: Full-printable and hole transport material (HTM)-free perovskite solar cells (PSCs) with carbon counter electrodes feature high stability and low cost. However, the perovskite film prepared by conventional one-step solution-coating method always shows a relatively poor coverage on the substrate, leading to the limit of the photocurrent density. In this study, we incorporated carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in the perovskite films, and investigated their effects on the performance of TiO2 nanosheet-based and HTM-free PSCs. It was found that the addition of CQDs to the perovskite film can enhance the photocurrent density of PSCs, and the optimal PSC with 0.1% CQDs evolved 60% higher photocurrent density than the pristine one. The improved photocurrent density was attributed to the heterogeneous nuclei derived from CQDs during perovskite crystallization, which would increase amount of perovskite nuclei and form a fine perovskite grain, leading to a better coverage on the substrate. Moreover, due to the excellent conductivity, CQDs in perovskite films could efficiently transport the photo-excited electrons, accelerating the separation and mobilization of charge carriers. This study presents the incorporation of CQDs in perovskite as an efficient approach to promote the performance of HTM-free PSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskite solar cells (PSCs)
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Film coverage
TiO2 nanosheets
Photocurrent density

xocs:title-editors-group.ce:title: 2nd International Workshop on Graphene and C3N4-based Photocatalysts

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of perovskite precursor incorporating CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM (a) and TEM (b) images of the obtained TiO2 nanosheets. (c) TEM image of the obtained CQDs, the inset is the optical photograph of CQD aqueous solution under UV irradiation (λ=365nm). (d) Raman spectrum of CQDs.
SEM images of the perovskite films derived from the precursor with different CQD contents: pristine (a), C0.05 (b), C0.1(c) and C0.5 (d).
XRD patterns of FTO-glass substrate, pristine perovskite and CQDs/perovskite films.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of pristine perovskite films, CQDs/perovskite films and CQDs. (b) Steady-state PL quenching spectra of pristine perovskite and CQDs/perovskite films.

(a) Schematic diagram of the structure of the full-printable PSC. (b) Cross-sectional SEM image of a typical PSC in this study. (c) Energy levels (relative to vacuum) of different layer of PSCs in this study.

(a) Nyquist plots of pristine and CQDs/perovskite solar cells under the dark condition at bias of −0.6V. Inset is the equivalent circuit. (b) Tafel plots of the pristine and CQDs/perovskite solar cells in dark.
(a) J-V curves of the pristine perovskite and CQDs/perovskite solar cells. (b) IPCE spectra of the pristine and C0.1-based PSCs.
Photovoltaic parameters of PSCs based on C0.05, C0.1, C0.5 and pristine perovskite films. (The average results are derived from the 10 randomly selected devices).

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocurrent density of HTM-free perovskite solar cells by carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Material Synthesis and Processing, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Material Synthesis and Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, King Abdulaziz University, Jeddah, 21589, Saudi Arabia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Material Synthesis and Processing, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Material Synthesis and Processing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Full-printable and hole transport material (HTM)-free perovskite solar cells (PSCs) with carbon counter electrodes feature high stability and low cost. However, the perovskite film prepared by conventional one-step solution-coating method always shows a relatively poor coverage on the substrate, leading to the limit of the photocurrent density. In this study, we incorporated carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in the perovskite films, and investigated their effects on the performance of TiO2 nanosheet-based and HTM-free PSCs. It was found that the addition of CQDs to the perovskite film can enhance the photocurrent density of PSCs, and the optimal PSC with 0.1% CQDs evolved 60% higher photocurrent density than the pristine one. The improved photocurrent density was attributed to the heterogeneous nuclei derived from CQDs during perovskite crystallization, which would increase amount of perovskite nuclei and form a fine perovskite grain, leading to a better coverage on the substrate. Moreover, due to the excellent conductivity, CQDs in perovskite films could efficiently transport the photo-excited electrons, accelerating the separation and mobilization of charge carriers. This study presents the incorporation of CQDs in perovskite as an efficient approach to promote the performance of HTM-free PSCs.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy conversion of sunlight to electricity is critical to renewable energy and sustainable for economic development without negative impact for environment [1]. Solar cell is regarded as one of the efficient and economical photovoltaics for energy conversion [2–5]. Recently, hybrid organic-inorganic metal halide perovskite solar cells (PSCs), which had shown impressively high power conversion efficiency (PCE) beyond 20% [6,7], attracted much attention worldwide. However, it is noted that hole transport materials (HTMs) are always needed in PSCs, meanwhile noble metals of gold and silver are normally used as counter electrode (CE) [8]. The conventional HTMs (e.g. spiro-OMeTAD) not only are expensive, but also restrict the long-term stability of PSCs. Furthermore, high vacuum deposition process for preparation of the noble metal CE is costly and energy-consuming, limiting the commercialization of PSCs dramatically. Therefore, HTM-free PSCs with low-cost carbon-based CE are developed to address these problems, and some of them have exhibited competitive energy conversion efficiencies and long-term stability [7–12].

Normally, to fabricate a low-cost and HTM-free PSCs, a TiO2 scaffold layer, a ZrO2 insulating layer and a porous carbon-based CE are successively prepared by screen printing, then a perovskite precursor is dropped on the carbon-based CE and infiltrates into the aforementioned three layers by one-step solution-coating method, which is considered as the simplest way to fabricate the device and thus becomes promising for future industrialization [13]. However, this method is inclined to cause micrometer-sized islands of perovskite grains and a large area of voids in the resulted perovskite film, leading to a poor coverage on TiO2 layer [14] and reduced light harvesting [15]. Although various approaches have been tried to control the crystal growth kinetics during the perovskite crystallization to obtain high-quality perovskite films, such as two-step sequential deposition [16,17], vacuum thermal evaporation [18–20] and solution-based precipitation-crystallization [21–24]. However, these methods are complicated, high-cost and time-consuming compared to the one-step solution-coating method. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop a convenient and efficient method to improve the coverage of perovskite film.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a novel class of carbon material with a size below 10nm. Because of their efficient electron transportation abilities, unique electron reservoir properties and nontoxicity [25,26], CQDs have been applied for photovoltaics [27,28], chemical sensing [29], photocatalysis [30–33], and light emitting diodes [34]. Herein, by incorporating CQDs in the one-step solution-coating process, we developed a simple and effective method to significantly enhance the coverage of the perovskite film on TiO2 nanosheet layer, and fabricated HTM-free mesoscopic PSCs through a screen-printing technique. The corresponding PSCs exhibited significantly improved short-circuit current densities (JSC ) and PCEs consequently comparing with the CQDs-free one, elucidating a great potential of CQDs for the high-quality perovskite films and improvement of PSCs.

Anatase TiO2 nanosheet is synthesized by the method reported previously [35–37]. Typically, 25mL tetrabutyl titanate (TBT, 98%, Tianjin yuanli Chemical Co., Ltd) and 3mL hydrofluoric acid solution (ca. 40wt%) were blended in a 100mL Teflon autoclave, followed by a hydrothermal reaction at 180°C for 24h. The obtained TiO2 nanosheets were separated by centrifugation, rinsed with deionized water and ethanol for three times, and dried in an oven overnight.

The preparation of CQDs followed the previous report [38]. Briefly, 8g NaOH and 40mL acetone were mixed under magnetic stirring for 1h and aged for 120h. The pH value of the mixture was tuned to neutral by 1M HCl. The product was collected by high-speed centrifugation and rinsed with distilled water. The precipitate was dried in an oven at 100°C for 10h to yield CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of perovskite precursor incorporating CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A pristine perovskite precursor solution was prepared as follows: 0.4g methylammonium iodide (CH3NH3I, Wuhan Jingge Solar Energy Technology Co., Ltd) and 1.146g lead iodide (PbI2, 99.999%, Wuhan Jingge Solar Energy Technology Co., Ltd) were dissolved in 2mL anhydrous N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, Sigma-Aldrich Corp.). To prepare CQD-containing perovskite films (CQDs/perovskite films), CQDs were added to the above perovskite precursor solution to reach mass ratios of 0.05%, 0.1%, and 0.5% (respective to the CH3NH3I) and the consequently obtained perovskite films were denoted as C0.05, C0.1, and C0.5, respectively.

A titanium diisopropoxide bis(acetylacetonate) (Sigma-Aldrich Corp.) solution (0.15M in 1-butanol) was spin-coated on pre-cleaned conductive fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass substrates at 2500rpm for 30s and heated at 125°C for 5min to make a dense TiO2 blocking layer. This spin-coating process was repeated twice. Then TiO2 nanosheets was screen-printed on the TiO2 blocking layer as the electron transport layer, followed by annealing at 450°C for 30min [39]. A ZrO2 space layer [39] was coated on the top of the TiO2 nanosheet layer, which serves as an insulating layer to block electrons flowing back to the counter electrode. To prepare the carbon-based counter electrode, a carbon black/graphite composite slurry [40] was printed on the ZrO2 layer and sintered at 400°C for 30min. Afterwards, the perovskite precursor solution with or without CQDs was dropped on the carbon layer, and infiltrated from the carbon layer to TiO2 layer by one-step solution-coating method. The sample was moved to a hot plate and heated at 70°C for 30min to complete the fabrication of PSC.

The morphologies of TiO2 nanosheets and perovskite films were investigated by a JSM-7500F field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were performed on a D/MAX-RB X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). The optical absorption properties of the pristine perovskite and CQDs/perovskite films were measured by an UV–vis spectrometer (UV2550, Shimadzu, Japan). The incident monochromatic photoelectric conversion efficiency (IPCE) was obtained by Newport's QE/IPCE Measurement Kit with a monochromatic light from 300W Xe lamp (Newport, model no.6258). The photocurrent-voltage (J-V) characteristics and the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were obtained by electrochemical work station (CHI660C Instrument, Chenhua Instrument Crop., China) under simulated 100mWcm−2 intensity (1 sun) by a solar simulator (91160, Newport Corp., Irvine, CA,USA), and the active cell area was 0.14cm2. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy were measured in dark condition with the frequency range of 0.01Hz–105 Hz. Steady state photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan) with 460nm excitation wavelength. Raman spectrum of CQDs was recorded with a micro-Raman spectrometer (RenishawInVia).

TiO2 nanosheet has been proven an efficient electron transport material for PSCs [39]. Thus in this study, we chose TiO2 nanosheet as the material of electron transport layer to support perovskite films. Fig. 1 a and b present the SEM and TEM images of the synthesized TiO2 nanosheets, respectively. The obtained TiO2 shows a well-defined nanosheet-shaped structure with a size of ca. 50–80nm. Fig. 1c exhibits the TEM image of the obtained CQDs with uniform diameter of 3–5nm. The inset of Fig. 1c shows the optical photograph of CQDs aqueous solution under irradiation with UV light (λ=365nm), the bright green fluorescence is attributed to the quantum size effect and the surface functional groups of CQDs [27]. These investigations indicate the successful preparation of CQDs. Fig. 1d exhibits the Raman spectrum of CQDs. Two broad peaks at ∼1330 and 1600cm−1 can be observed, which are derived from the D-band (sp3) and G-band (sp2) of CQDs, respectively. The ratio of D-band/G-band intensity (ID/IG) for the CQDs is ∼0.7, which indicates the highly amorphous form of the prepared CQDs [41].

The SEM images in Fig. 2 show that the pristine precursor solution without CQDs results in a dendritic structure of crystals and a poor coverage of the perovskite film (Fig. 2a), leading to inefficient charge transport and poor light harvesting, which will be discussed in the following sections. In contrast, when a small amount of CQDs are added to the perovskite precursor solution, the size of perovskite crystals and voids in the obtained films can be significantly decreased (Fig. 2b and c). Obviously, C0.1 film with only 0.1% CQD content has already exhibited an almost full coverage over the underlying TiO2 nanosheet layer (Fig. 2c). By increasing the CQDs content to 0.5% in the perovskite precursor solution, the grain size of perovskite of the obtained film is further reduced with increased grain boundaries (C0.5, Fig. 2d).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were carried out to further investigate the role of CQD additive in the crystallization of the perovskite films. As shown in Fig. 3 , intense diffraction peaks at 14.08°, 23.48°, 28.40°, and 31.86° indexed to (110), (211), (220) and (310) diffractions of the tetragonal CH3NH3PbI3 phase can be observed in the XRD patterns of all the perovskite-based films, which are consistent with the previous report [42]. Perovskite films present almost the same XRD pattern independent of the CQD additive content. Notably, the pristine perovskite film exhibits the sharpest diffraction peaks with strong (110) preferred orientation, implying relatively large-size grains and good crystallinity of the perovskite. On the other hand, with increasing the CQD content, the diffraction peaks become gradually wide and weak, indicating decreased perovskite grain size. This result matches with the SEM characterization (Fig. 2). Generally, the process of perovskite precursor precipitation contains two steps: nucleation and crystal growth. Perovskite nuclei will be formed when the precursor solution reaches supersaturation. The number of the perovskite nuclei is based on the rate of nucleation and the degree of the supersaturation. In our case, due to the low vapor pressure (2.6mmHg at 20°C) and high boiling point (153°C) of DMF [15], the drying process of the film is relatively slow, which leads to a low degree of supersaturation and a small quantity of perovskite nuclei, accelerating the crystal growth of the existing nuclei and formation of micrometer-sized dendritic perovskite with large voids in the films, as shown in Fig. 2a. However, a small content of CQDs in the precursor solution can act as the heterogeneous nuclei, increase the number of perovskite nuclei during the perovskite crystallization, which will suppress the growth of individual perovskite crystal, leading to fine perovskite grains and providing a better coverage on TiO2 layer with much smaller and fewer voids in the film, as indicated in Fig. 2b–d.

UV-vis absorption spectra of the obtained perovskite films are shown in Fig. 4 a. Comparing with the pristine perovskite film, C0.1 exhibits a significantly enhanced UV–vis absorption in a wide region (400–760nm), which is attributed to the better coverage of the perovskite film, as confirmed by SEM investigation (Fig. 2). However, when the CQD content increases to 0.5% in the perovskite precursor solution, the excess CQDs can cover the surface of perovskite crystals, thus hinder the light from being harvested by perovskite, resulting in a lower absorption. It is noted that the pristine perovskite film presents a slightly higher absorption in the wavelength range of 780–800nm, which should be due to the significant light scattering caused by the large perovskite crystals on the surface [43–46]. We also notice that the absorption spectra of the perovskite films incorporating CQDs exhibit obvious blue-shift with increasing CQD content, which agrees well with the steady-state photoluminescence (PL) quenching measurement (Fig. 4b), where the PL spectra of perovskite layers with CQDs are blue shifted compared to that of the pristine one. This occurrence is consistent with the recent reports [47,48], which demonstrated that small perovskite crystallites can lead to a blue-shifted emission, and the magnitude of the shift correlates inversely with the observed crystal size. Moreover, comparing with the pristine perovskite film, the PL intensity of perovskite films with CQD additive shows more efficient PL quenching, indicating better electron extraction ability. CQDs can serve as a superfast electron tunnel, resulting in a strong quenching of perovskite absorber [28]. The reduced PL quenching intensity with CQD additive verifies the suppressed carrier recombination and decreased trap state defects in perovskite films [49]. From the above investigations, it is confirmed that as the heterogeneous nuclei for the perovskite crystallization, the added CQDs in the perovskite precursor solution can lead to the smaller grain size, better film coverage and lower carrier recombination for the perovskite films.

Fig. 5 a presents the structure of the full-printable HTM-free PSCs in our case. From the cross-section SEM image in Fig. 5b, the triple layers (TiO2 nanosheet layer, ZrO2 insulation layer and carbon CE layer) can be clearly observed. As can be seen in Fig. 5c, the energy levels of the conduction band edges of TiO2, CQD and CH3NH3PbI3 are −4.1, −4.01, and −3.9eV, respectively [50]. While the valence band edge of the CH3NH3PbI3 is around −5.4eV [28], and the work function of carbon is at −5.0eV. These energy level alignments allow a preferable flow of charge carrier through PSCs. The perovskite CH3NH3PbI3 layer absorbs incident light to generate photo-exited electrons and holes on the conduction band and valence band, respectively. The excited electrons inject into the conduction band of CQDs instantly, and then transport to the mesoporous TiO2 nanosheet layer. Meanwhile, the holes on the valence band transfer to the carbon counter electrode. Therefore, the generated photoelectrons and holes can be efficiently separated, and effective photocurrent can be produced by their directional migrations in PSCs.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted under dark condition to investigate the electron recombination at the interfaces in PSCs. Usually, two arcs deriving from two charge transfer processes in PSCs can be observed in EIS: A small arc in the high frequency region represents the charge transfer resistance at perovskite/carbon CE interface; A large arc at lower frequency region stems from the resistance of charge recombination between mesoscopic TiO2 and the perovskite layer [51]. However, as shown in Fig. 6 a, in our case, only a single distinct arc in the low frequency region can be detected, which is assigned to the very small charge transfer resistance at the interface of perovskite/carbon CE [52]. It is found that adding CQD to perovskite can enlarge the arc at low frequency region comparing with the pristine one, while C0.1-based PSC possesses the largest arc, indicating that its recombination resistance at the TiO2/perovskite interface is highest among PSCs and resulting in lowest electron recombination. As aforementioned, a small amount of CQDs act as heterogeneous nuclei and enhance the nucleation during the perovskite crystallization, which would reduce defects of perovskite films and strengthen the interconnection of the perovskite and the TiO2 nanosheet surface, contributing to the remarkably increased recombination resistance. Further increasing the CQD content beyond 0.1% leads to a decreased recombination resistance, probably because the high-content CQDs become the charge recombination centers and increase the grain boundaries of perovskite crystal, which increases the probability of recombination of holes and electrons [53,54].

Tafel polarization measurements of the PSCs were conducted on dark condition as well, as shown in Fig. 6b. The exchange current density (J0 ) can be obtained by the intersection of the extrapolated linear cathodic or anodic branches. The value of J0 are in the order of C0.1 (3.15μAcm−2)<C0.05 (4.60μAcm−2)<C0.5 (6.32μAcm−2)<Pristine (10.76μAcm−2), and a larger J0 indicates a higher leakage current of PSCs. Comparing with the pristine PSC, the leakage current of the ones incorporating CQDs are obviously suppressed, which means the decreased recombination at the interface of TiO2 nanosheet/perovskite. Among all the samples, C0.1-based PSC shows the smallest J0 and therefore is supposed to own the lowest leakage current and most efficient electron transfer. These results are consistent with the investigation of the EIS (Fig. 6a).

The current densities-voltage (J-V) characteristics were performed to investigate the impact of CQDs on the performance of HTM-free PSCs, as shown in Fig. 7 a and Table 1 . Apparently, all the PSCs in this study have a similar open-circuit voltage (VOC ) of ∼0.79V, therefore the differences in performance are mainly attributed to the short-circuit current densities (JSC ) (Table 1). The tendency of JSC is in the order of C0.1>C0.05>C0.5>pristine, which is in accordance with our UV, PL, EIS and Tafel analyses. Notably, the PSCs incorporating CQDs have higher JSC than the pristine one, resulting in better PCEs. The pristine PSC exhibits a VOC of 0.79V and a JSC of 10.20mAcm−2, resulting in a relatively low PCE of 4.4%. In contrast, the optimized CQD content of 0.1% (C0.1) can lead to a striking enhancement of JSC to 16.40mAcm−2, consequently a PCE of 7.62% can be obtained, which is ∼60% higher than that of the pristine one. Moreover, the incident photo to current efficiency (IPCE) of C0.1-based PSC shows an apparent enhancement in the entire spectrum (400–800nm) (Fig. 7b), agreeing very well with its higher JSC . From the above investigations, it is concluded that CQDs can benefit the perovskite film by a better coverage and fewer voids, leading to enhanced light harvesting efficiency, less electron recombination and improved performance of HTM-free PSCs.

In summary, we have demonstrated a facile and efficient strategy to enhance the performance of full-printable and HTMs-free PSCs via incorporating CQDs. The prepared CQDs involved PSCs show much improved JSC and PCEs comparing with the pristine one. Particularly, the optimal 0.1% CQDs incorporated PSC exhibits the highest JSC (16.40mAcm−2), which is ∼60% higher than the pristine one (10.20mAcm−2). It is believed that a small amount of CQDs in the perovskite precursor could act as the heterogeneous nuclei during the perovskite crystallization, which increase the number of perovskite nuclei and lead to a better coverage of the substrate. In addition, the CQDs in the perovskite films could efficiently transport the photogenerated electrons, which could reduce the charge carrier recombination. These merits would significantly improve the JSC of PSCs. This study not only shows the CQD as an additive for potential morphology control and electron transport in perovskite films, but also paves an avenue to make high-performance PSCs by incorporating quantum dots materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by NSFC (21433007, 51372190, 21573170 and 51320105001), 973 Program (2013CB632402), NSFHP (2015CFA001), Innovative Research Funds of SKLWUT (2017-ZD-4) and Fundamental Research Funds of Central Universities (WUT: 2015-III-034).
